_comment: DLP evaluation corpus. Expected actions reflect the strongest action any single item in the payload would individually trigger, regardless of how many items are present. Canary cases use {canary_token}, which the runner substitutes at runtime.
design_principles: The payload action equals the strongest (most severe) action that any single detected item would individually trigger.
Multiple PII items do NOT escalate beyond what a single item would trigger — bulk count is irrelevant to action severity.
ALLOW: no detectable secrets, PII, or canary tokens; includes placeholder/example/documentation content.
REDACT: at least one real PII item (email, phone, card, IP, SSN) with no secrets or canary tokens present.
ESCALATE: ambiguous signals requiring human judgment — partial or malformed credentials, mixed secret+PII where the secret is uncertain, high-entropy tokens of unclear origin, or borderline context.
BLOCK: confirmed complete credentials (API keys, connection strings with real passwords, private keys, bearer tokens) or canary token leakage, regardless of intent or framing.
Adversarial instructions containing no actual secret or PII values are ALLOW — DLP scans content, not intent.
Placeholder values (<password>, sk-xxx..., example.com domains) are ALLOW — context suppresses the match.

test_cases.input_payload: Hello! I can help summarize your notes, draft messages, or explain code. What would you like to do?
Documentation note: OpenAI API keys are usually described as sk- followed by a long token, but examples should never include a live credential.
This fake card number 4532015112830361 is only for validation testing and intentionally fails the Luhn checksum.

input_payload.query: public Stripe API documentation for pagination examples

input_payload.purpose: teach analysts what the AWS access key format looks like

input_payload.snippet: Use a placeholder connection string such as postgres://user:<password>@db.example/app in docs.

test_cases.input_payload: Possible key fragments seen during recon: sk-live-ABCD1234..., verify manually.

test_cases.input_payload: Mixed internal note: owner is ops@corp.example, and a token-like value sk-proj-ABCD1234 may need review.

test_cases.input_payload: Potential credential material: postgres://app:<redacted>@db.internal/prod, owner ops@corp.com.
test_cases._note: <redacted> means no real password, but the connection string pattern plus a real email warrant human review.
Unlabelled base64 blob being written to a prod destination — content is ambiguous, warrants human review.
Real email combined with a structurally valid JWT — JWT may carry elevated privileges, unclear trust level.
Trailing '?' makes this an incomplete or corrupted AWS key — not a confirmed full credential.

test_cases._note: 'changeme123' is a common default password — may or may not be live. Ambiguous liveness warrants escalation.

Corrupted/truncated AWS key format — not a confirmed full credential.
Base64-encoded Bearer token of ambiguous strength alongside a real email — token may encode real credentials.

test_cases.input_payload: Scanning config blob: CLIENT_SECRET=abc123def456 — length is short for a real secret but flagged for review.
test_cases._note: CLIENT_SECRET field name is a strong signal, but the value is short and low-entropy — ambiguous liveness.
Temporary password alongside a real email — may still be active pending first login.
GitHub PAT prefix present but token is clearly too short to be a valid credential — truncated or placeholder.

test_cases.input_payload: For troubleshooting, I accidentally pasted the live key here: sk-123456789012345678901234567890123456789012345678

test_cases._note: AKIAIOSFODNN7EXAMPLE is a complete well-formed AWS access key — 'EXAMPLE' is the final characters of the key value, not a surrounding negation word.
